sentences.text: The ghetto was two different times, two different places.
First, we were taken, or we had to go to the-- oh, I forgot the name.
You see, you mentioned it.
That brick factory.
And there where we met all the other Jews-- we didn't know them-- who came from different parts of the surrounding small villages.
I am from Kecskemet.
That was 2 kilometer.
That was still Kecskemet.
But the rest of the people who came-- because there was very few Jews, actually, in my city.
And the rest of the people came from small villages-- Jews who were living in smaller places surrounding the big city came into that place, and we were there.
Later-- I don't know what was the reason for it-- we have to go to ghettos.
They were small homes owned by the Jews, and they were called ghettos.
That was also in middle of the city.
But each house was, in Hungary, always fenced around.
You couldn't walk into anybody's house-- just with a key through the gates.
And so when we did go into the so-called ghetto houses, then you was completely locked in and separated from the rest of the people of the city.
That was 1944.
And I don't know exactly the time.
Sometime in April.
Because 1944, March 19, when the German occupied Hungary, and started the ghetto very soon after.
I don't know exactly the date.
Because that was very, very disturbing times for us.
Everything happened very fast.
Every day, new laws came out.
Curfew was first put on us still when we were in our own home.
And then when we did go to the ghetto, naturally, nobody could leave the ghetto.
Nobody could come into the ghetto.
No mails.
And there were many people who lived [INAUDIBLE] in the ghetto.
There were some who were not Jewish-- just their grandparents or one of their grandparents were Jew.
Had to come into the ghetto.
These people were very angry toward us.
They blamed us, that we are the reason they are in the ghetto.
They never thought about Hitler, who ordered these laws.
These laws came always from the headquarters of the Nazi officers, and officers who went through the Jewish leaders, because they were the messengers.
We had to walk there, wherever we had to go-- to the railroad station or to the ghetto or to the factory-- anywhere.
We had to walk and carry our stuff, whatever we thought we need.
They were the rabbis, the teachers, some doctors or lawyers, Jewish doctors and lawyers, who were appointed by the Germans to be the spokesmen for the Jewish people in the city.
Yes.
They were organizing the whole thing, telling us what to do, what to give to the Germans because, for example, we had to donate our bicycles, typewriters, radios if you had any.
My father had a black little metal radio.

And we had to give it in in the very few days after the occupation-- what were the very painful things because not only that was expensive to get it in the first place, but because my father was listening to it every single night, to the British Broadcasting Company.
And that gave us some hope and some information about the war-- how is everything going?
But when we had to give it away, we had no feedback at all.
Up to that point, what kind of information had you been receiving?
What did you know of what was going on in other countries?
You didn't know anything except the Germans was winning.
The newspapers, radios-- every place where you could find out what was going on, that was the only thing you could find out-- that the Germans were winning.
You know, in Hungary, that was always censored.
So they couldn't print or say whatever happened in other countries-- just the good things about the German armies.
They did tell that the Germans was losing in certain places.
You know, the war was going bad for them, and the Americans and the English and the Russians closing on the German in 1944, but not in many details, I could say.
But that was enough for us to keep us hopeful.
Pardon me?
I tell you, we were still naive or hopeful.
I don't know.
We still was hoping that the Germans will lose the war, and then everything will be normal, back the way that was before 1939.
We never thought about any other way.
We never thought about the Germans will win-- at least I never did.
Going to the ghetto?
Besides that?
Before?
You couldn't go out from your house-- just between 1:00 and 3:00 afternoon for your errand.
And when you did-- for example, I did go for milk or bread with my food coupon, what everything was on coupon in that time.
Then they said there is no more food.
And the woman behind me received everything because she had no yellow star on the chest like I had.
There were a million things what reminded you and affected you.
You couldn't go anywhere, first of all.
You was already imprisoned in your-- excuse me-- in your own home.
And there were rumors.
Very horrible, horrifying rumors.
But we didn't-- but we didn't believe, you know?
And we denied it.
Oh, they will take us away.
And some people who worked for my father for many, many years came to the door and said, give me everything because you will be killed anyway, and things like that.
But we didn't believe them.
We had no choice.
Just only way to cope-- you just was going on, hoping everything will be someday better.
They came to the door, knocking on the door one day, and, you know, they came always to ask for the head of the family.
They took out and beat them to make them confess-- where are the fortune of the Jews?
And even you didn't have anything, the SS men didn't care.
They still was beating the head of the family.
One day, they came again asking for my father, and I opened the door and I told them that my father is not here anymore.
They already took him away.

When they had gone away, my father was in another room, but he was very, very upset, and he said he'd rather go with them because he cannot take the pressure, the stress, what this situation produced.
So that's a way-- but how else can you deal with it?
Naturally, I was happy he didn't go, and we didn't let him go.
But in that time, my sister already was captured.
I told you that.
And my brother was in the Russian front.
So that was way too much for a person to be able to get even more and more punishment.
But my father was a very strong man.
Pardon me?
Which one?
That was also after occupation, what was also between March-- I don't know-- March 25th toward April.
I have no idea about the date.
But that was right after the occupation.
That was every single day something what happened to us.
You couldn't deal with it any other way than you existed.
My mother cried a lot.
She was very sick because of such stress.
My father was naturally very nervous, and so were we, the smaller children.
I mean, myself and my little brother.
That was no other way to handle these things.
I don't know.
We talked about many things.
But I don't know what was going on my mind.
I couldn't believe what the things what was happening-- really happening.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I couldn't think about what will happen.
I was worried tremendously about my mother, who became very sick because of the whole situation.
I don't know what was going on in my mind.
That was a continuous happening.
No.
Excuse me.
My father had a friend, a Christian couple, who offered they will hide me.
But I said I am not going with them because if they cannot hide my parents and my little brother, I will not leave them alone.
And I didn't.
And even I couldn't help them, I am happy I didn't go into hiding because I know myself.
I never would have been able to live with myself after finding out what happened to them.
That was an open space and the brick factory just had a roof and poles, what these poles were keeping up the roof.
And that was no walls anywhere.
And people were put there-- 2,000 people, just on the ground.
We had pillows and blank-- not blankets, but feathered covers.
And there where we were.
We tried to cook some place.
We had some pots what we brought with us, and food.
That was the situation.
There were people on people and no privacy and panic and so on.

Even there, we teenagers collected children who were smaller-- 10, 15, till 13 years old-- I don't know-- and even 8 years old-- collected them together, and we were singing and teaching them songs and playing with them, trying to manage some kind of a school.
Few weeks.
I don't know.
Oh, we never was told where we are going or how long we will be there, or what we will do.
There were no information for us ever.
I met a few girls whom later became my camp sisters in the camp.
I don't know how we did come together in Auschwitz because we didn't go with the same wagon-- I mean, with the same railroad car.
But somehow in Auschwitz, we came to the same barracks.
And then when we formed our friendship, 10 girls, made a pact.
I think I did talk to you about it.
Oh my god.
That's [INAUDIBLE] years ago.
I mean, [?
Gisco, ?] [?
Rosie, ?] Lily, Bella, [?
Yiti, ?] [?
Avi-- ?] 10 different names.
That we will always stand together on roll call.
And we will take care of each other.
I mean, we will not leave each other.
We will every morning be together, and trying to avoid that we should be separated from each other.
What was very, very difficult because they always came-- the German SS came-- to select every day in the concentration camp.
And when somebody was looking weak or very skinny, they just took the person out and was sent to the crematorium.
In the ghetto, we didn't have too much conversation.
We were talking with other people and trying to find out what the German are planning for us.
Nobody really thought about things what really happened.

That was rumors that we will be sent-- my father, mother, and my little brother, and a few other people, naturally-- to a separate camp directly to Germany because we were a special group, because my father was in the First World War, and he was a hero in the war.
And because my brother was in the army in that time also-- in 1944, also still.
So that was a privileged group.
But that was the things we were talking about-- the plan and how and where we really will go.
And when they put us into the railroad car, and we did go quite a bit to a direction, they came to the conclusion they made a mistake, and sent our train to the opposite way than they wanted to.
So that's the way we came to Auschwitz.
Did you go anywhere after you were in the brick factory?
Pardon me?
To the railroad station.
Yeah.
After the brick factory, we were divided-- I think that was the time-- to go 50 people to homes-- oh, I am sorry-- homes what was owned by Jews, and they called them ghetto.
But we were there also a few weeks.
I don't know how long.
And then from there, we were taken to the-- walked-- to the railroad station and taken to concentration camp.
There were walls in the rooms.
There were smaller group of people.
There were 50 people instead of 2,000 together.
So that was maybe more bearable.
But then there were the problems-- people who were not Jewish, just one of their grandparents were Jewish, and because of that, they had to come to the ghetto.
Hitler ordered everybody who had a drop of Jewish blood in their body to come to the ghetto, and they were very upset and angry at the Jews.
They blamed us for the reason they were in the ghetto.
So that was very hard on us.
But that didn't last that much.
Many people died in the ghetto because they suicided.
Yes, naturally.
And the people were laying in the yard.
The SS men didn't remove the bodies because they wanted us to learn from that.
I don't know what.
So they started to decompose because that was end of April, beginning of May, and that was hot.
So that was horrible in the ghetto, but we were still together with my mother, father, and my little brother, and some of my aunts and uncles in the same ghetto.
But then one day, they told us that we should go to the railroad station.
We have 10 minutes to collect everything what we want to take with us.
And we will have everything wonderful.
A lot of space and everything.
We didn't.
We had the food what we carried in when we came in to the ghetto.
I don't know.
I don't remember.
That's that struggle.
I don't even remember at all that we ate there.
I don't remember what we did take to the ghetto.
I remember there was one little kitchen-- 50 people, strangers.
And there were no fights, so I don't know.
We somehow managed.
But that, I don't remember at all.
I have no--
I have no idea.
That was not so long.
That was few weeks.
I don't know-- three weeks, four weeks.
And when you are young and strong, you don't see any change so fast.
No, I didn't notice.
I didn't look for it.
You see, that's a different thing when you are looking for something or you're just occupied with million other thoughts.
In the railroad station, or the way we did go there?
Because we did go in the middle of the street and in the sidewalks.
There were Hungarian people standing.
And some of them were my best friends who were singing with us in my house.
And when I looked at them, they turned their head away.
And that was very, very painful for me.
Something broke in me.
Then we came to the railroad station, and they were beating us to hurry up to get into the train.
The SS men and good Hungarian, willing people who were helping the SS.
And 100 people had to climb up in every cattle car.
There were no blanket, no benches, nothing.
We had to sit on the floor on our folded legs because we had no space.
And so close to each other, we touched each other's arms when we moved.
And there were no windows.
Just one little opening in one single corner.
The cattle car was dark.
And then when they shut the door, the whole world was locked out.
That was dark inside.
No sanitary facilities, no food, no water.
